front: Period: this name is synonymous with a dark red/purple color, associated with its namesake wine
Period: in the 15th century, ruled by a cadet branch of the Valois dynasty under dukes John the Fearless, Philip the Good, and Charles the Bold, who often changed sides during the Hundred Years' War
Period: a dynasty with this name was first to use a cross of five blue shields with white dots, a design still used on Portugal's flag
Period: took its name from a post-Roman Germanic people led by Godomar and Gundobad, the latter of whom issued the namesake law code (the Lex Burgundionum) in the 5th century CE
Event: this early Burgundian kingdom saw a civil war between King Gundobad and his brother Godegesil
Period: historian Norman Davies identified fifteen European polities to bear this name; the first was established under King Gunther at Worms in the 5th century
Period: an upper and lower state with this name were merged into a medieval kingdom sometimes called Arelat within the Holy Roman Empire, capitaled at Arles
Ruler: after her husband Clovis II's death, ruled a united Austrasia, Neustria, and Burgundy, which was later divided between East and West Francia by the Treaty of Meerssen

Institution: for several hundred years, part of Burgundy's lands formed this "Free County" due to rights won by ruler Reginald III; it and the Duchy agreed to mutual neutrality in 1522 at Saint-Jean-de-Losne
Event: created in 1363 when King John II of France granted the territory to his son Philip the Bold; historian Johan Huizinga wrote of the enduring chivalric myth surrounding its founding
work: Philip the Bold and the Founding of the Valois Duchy

front: Ruler: as regent for young King Charles VI, dismissed a group of royal councilors nicknamed the "Marmosets"; the duchy had earlier been occupied by mercenary captain Arnaut de Cervole, the "Archpriest"
work: Philip the Bold and the Founding of the Valois Duchy

front: Ruler: personal motto "il me tarde" ("I look forward") appeared on his seal; protected Pierre de Créon after Créon stole 100,000 francs from the Duke of Anjou
work: Philip the Bold and the Founding of the Valois Duchy

front: Ruler: ordered construction of the Chartreuse de Champmol, a Carthusian monastery, as a burial site for his dynasty
work: Philip the Bold and the Founding of the Valois Duchy

front: Sculpture: created by Claus Sluter for the cloister of the Chartreuse de Champmol; depicts Jeremiah wearing copper glasses, along with David, Isaiah, and Daniel
work: Philip the Bold and the Founding of the Valois Duchy

front: Ruler: arranged a 1385 double wedding at Cambrai in which a prince and princess from Burgundy married a princess and prince from Bavaria
work: Philip the Bold and the Founding of the Valois Duchy

front: Ruler: had his cousin Louis of Orléans hacked to death by street thugs in 1407, then allegedly stole his mistress, Queen Isabeau
Ruler: enlisted theologian Jean Petit to publicly defend the Orléans assassination he had perpetrated, which triggered the Cabochien Revolt
Event: civil war sparked by John the Fearless's 1407 assassination of Louis of Orléans, pitting Burgundians against a faction led by Bernard VII of Armagnac
Ruler: seized Paris after the Battle of Agincourt
Ruler: murdered on the bridge at Montereau by agents of the Dauphin (future Charles VII) in 1419, in revenge for the Orléans assassination
Institution: founded by Philip the Good to celebrate his marriage to Isabella of Portugal
Event: 1454 banquet hosted by Philip the Good to drum up support for a crusade against the Ottomans after their capture of Constantinople, though no crusade ever took place
Ruler: patronized painter Jan van Eyck from 1425 until the artist's death, as well as composer Guillaume Dufay
Institution: almshouse in Burgundy's namesake Romanesque style, founded by Philip the Good's Chancellor Nicolas Rolin
Ruler: won the last Hook and Cod War against Jacoba (Jacqueline) of Bavaria/Hainaut, who ceded three counties to him
Ruler: his forces captured Joan of Arc at Compiègne and handed her to his English allies, who put her on heresy trial
Document: 1435 treaty in which Philip the Good reallied with France's Charles VII, ending the Armagnac-Burgundian Civil War; his later support of the Praguerie revolt ended this alliance
Ruler: farmed out a substantial portion of his tax income to the Italian cloth merchant Giovanni Arnolfini
Ruler: regulated the quality of Burgundy's namesake dark red wine
Ruler: nicknamed "the Bold" (le Téméraire, more literally "the Rash"); the last Valois Duke of Burgundy
Ruler: refused to shave after losing a battle in which the "Three Brothers" jewelry was stolen from his tent
Event: alliance of French nobles formed by Charles the Bold (while Count of Charolais) against Louis XI, with Charles, Duke of Berry, as its nominal figurehead

Document: after Charles the Bold's victory at Montlhéry (1465), Louis XI was forced to sign this treaty, granting Charles control of Boulogne, Ponthieu, and Guînes and dissolving the League of the Public Weal
Ruler: put down several rebellions by the city of Liège and allied with England by marrying Margaret of York
Ruler: funded Edward IV's army before the Battle of Barnet as part of an English alliance; the Earl of Warwick partly broke with Edward IV over this alliance
Ruler: paid 300,000 florins to subsume the lands of Arnold of Egmont, Duke of Guelders, absorbing the territory after Arnold's death
Battle: 1477 defeat in which Charles the Bold died fighting Swiss and Lorraine forces allied with Louis XI (the "Spider-King"), ending independent Burgundian statehood
Ruler: Charles the Bold's only child and heir, who inherited the Burgundian title after his death at Nancy
work: Mary of Burgundy and the Habsburg Succession

front: Document: signed by Mary of Burgundy, restoring regional freedoms and abolishing the Parliament of Mechelen, after her succession was affirmed by the Joyous Entry of 1477
work: Mary of Burgundy and the Habsburg Succession

front: Ruler: married Mary of Burgundy as his first wife, an event that inspired the Habsburg saying "Let others wage war; you, happy Austria, marry"
work: Mary of Burgundy and the Habsburg Succession

front: Ruler: died in a riding accident; legendarily reappeared as a shade summoned by Johannes Trithemius in a Faust-like episode; her spouse fictionalized their courtship in the poem Theuerdank
work: Mary of Burgundy and the Habsburg Succession

front: Ruler: displayed Mary of Burgundy's infant son naked to a crowd to disprove rumors, spread by Louis XI (the "Universal Spider"), that the child was a girl
work: Mary of Burgundy and the Habsburg Succession

front: Document: settled the war over the Burgundian succession, ending hostilities over the Seventeen Provinces and confirming Mary of Burgundy's inheritance
work: Mary of Burgundy and the Habsburg Succession

front: Concept: flag of a red "X" (saltire) on a white background, used by the Spanish Habsburgs after the Burgundian succession
work: Mary of Burgundy and the Habsburg Succession

front: Document: this 1482 treaty gave Maximilian I the County of Flanders from the former Burgundian duchy, while the rest went to Louis XI's France
work: Mary of Burgundy and the Habsburg Succession

front: Institution: administrative division of the Holy Roman Empire organized from former Burgundian lands, including the "Generality Lands" pacified by the Guelders Wars

Concept: school of composers centered at Philip the Good's court, including Guillaume Dufay, Gilles Binchois, and later Antoine Busnois, known for polyphony and the technique of faux-bourdon (parallel first-inversion triads), partly inspired by intervals in John Dunstaple's works
work: Burgundian Art, Music, and Illuminated Manuscripts

front: Work: chanson whose musical settings by Burgundian-school composers were possibly prompted by a duke's call for a crusade at the Feast of the Pheasant
work: Burgundian Art, Music, and Illuminated Manuscripts

front: Concept: painters including Jan van Eyck and Rogier van der Weyden flourished at the Burgundian ducal court, often centered at this city
work: Burgundian Art, Music, and Illuminated Manuscripts

front: Artist: created miniature mourner statues for Burgundian ducal tombs
work: Burgundian Art, Music, and Illuminated Manuscripts

front: Region: historic capital is Dijon; in the 2010s this region was merged with its former "Free County," Franche-Comté, to form the official region of Bourgogne-Franche-Comté
Concept: monastery-developed wine-growing plots ("climats" and "terroirs") of this region comprise a UNESCO World Heritage Site
Food: beef stew made with carrots, onions, mushrooms, and Burgundy's namesake dry red wine
Food: an Easter dish from Burgundy, prepared by setting cubed pork shoulder and calf's-foot gelatin in a terrine
Food: cocktail of white wine and blackcurrant liqueur (crème de cassis), popularized by Félix Kir, mayor of Dijon
Food: soft cheese made in its namesake town in the Côte-d'Or department of Burgundy
